coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free and dispersant-free synthesis of narrow full width at half maximum carbon dots via sole precursor of p-phenylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Hongcan
Xu, Shuhong
Chen, Zengwei
Liu, Fan
Zong, Shenfei
Wang, Zhuyuan
Wang, Chunlei
coredata.dc:description: Narrow band gap emissive carbon dots (CDs), especially those with full width at half maximum (FWHM) below 40 nm, is extremely important for biological imaging application due to higher color purity and better resolution. The synthesis of narrow FWHM emissive CDs usually requires the use of specific-structured molecular precursors with a large π conjugated domain. In comparison, the most common precursors for CDs, for instance p-phenylenediamine, are usually believed unsuitable for preparing CDs with FWHM emission below 40 nm. In this work, we challenged this stereotype by using p-phenylenediamine precursor to synthesize CDs with a small FWHM of only 29 nm. Instead of using traditional solvothermal method, a facile solvent-free method was developed to synthesize CDs with the powder of p-phenylenediamine in the autoclave under a reaction temperature of 200 °C. After dispersed in solution, CDs show a red emission around 615 nm with FWHM of 29 nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 12.8%. Through experiments and theoretical calculation, we found that the FWHM emission is positive correlated to the content of amino groups on the surface of CDs. In comparison to traditional solvothermal method, the current solvent-free method can promote the synthesis of CDs with less amino groups on the surface, reducing fluorescence center types, and hence realizing smaller FWHM emission. In addition, as-prepared CDs were used for biological imaging, and realized a rapid no-wash cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Narrow FWHM emission
Solvent-free and dispersant-free synthesis
Gaussian calculation
Rapid no-wash cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs with a solvent-free and dispersant-free method (CDs-SF)
Preparation of CDs at present of H3BO3 dispersant
Preparation of CDs with solvothermal method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 22075043 , 21875034 , 61704093 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Variation trend of emission intensity of carbon dots synthesized under different (a) reaction temperature, (b) mass of precursor and (c) reaction time. (d) Emission and absorption spectra of CDs after dialysis for 12 h. (e) TEM (f) HRTEM of CDs-SF. (c) XRD spectrum.

(a) PL and PLE (λem=610 nm) spectroscopy of CDs-SF. (b) PL and PLE (λem=570 nm) spectroscopy of CDs-EA. (c) PL and PLE (λem=570 nm) spectroscopy of CDs-DMF. (d) PL and PLE (λem=610 nm) spectroscopy of CDs-H3BO3.

(a) XPS survey spectra of CDs-SF; (b) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs-SF. (c) XPS survey spectra of CDs-EA; (d) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs-EA. (e) XPS survey spectra of CDs-DMF; (f) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs-DMF. (g) XPS survey spectra of CDs-H3BO3; (h) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs-H3BO3.
FTIR for the different CDs samples, the red area indicates overtone of NH2 bending, while the blue area indicates aryl ring stretching vibration.
Configurations (a) and normalized spectra of molecules obtained with DFT (b).
The formation mechanism of the CDs synthesized by this solvent-free and dispersant-free method.
Imaging of MCF-7 cells by using solvent-free and dispersant-free CDs under a super-resolution microscope (λex=561 nm, λem=570-650 nm).
Solvent-free and dispersant-free synthetic strategy of narrow FWHM emissive carbon dots.
The relative contents of different composition of N for different CDs samples according to high-resolution N1s spectrum.

head.ce:title: Solvent-free and dispersant-free synthesis of narrow full width at half maximum carbon dots via sole precursor of p-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Photonics Center, School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Photonics Center, School of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Photonics Center, School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Narrow band gap emissive carbon dots (CDs), especially those with full width at half maximum (FWHM) below 40 nm, is extremely important for biological imaging application due to higher color purity and better resolution. The synthesis of narrow FWHM emissive CDs usually requires the use of specific-structured molecular precursors with a large π conjugated domain. In comparison, the most common precursors for CDs, for instance p-phenylenediamine, are usually believed unsuitable for preparing CDs with FWHM emission below 40 nm. In this work, we challenged this stereotype by using p-phenylenediamine precursor to synthesize CDs with a small FWHM of only 29 nm. Instead of using traditional solvothermal method, a facile solvent-free method was developed to synthesize CDs with the powder of p-phenylenediamine in the autoclave under a reaction temperature of 200 °C. After dispersed in solution, CDs show a red emission around 615 nm with FWHM of 29 nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 12.8%. Through experiments and theoretical calculation, we found that the FWHM emission is positive correlated to the content of amino groups on the surface of CDs. In comparison to traditional solvothermal method, the current solvent-free method can promote the synthesis of CDs with less amino groups on the surface, reducing fluorescence center types, and hence realizing smaller FWHM emission. In addition, as-prepared CDs were used for biological imaging, and realized a rapid no-wash cell imaging.

A facile solvent-free and dispersant-free method was developed to synthesize CDs with the powder of p-phenylenediamine in the autoclave under a reaction temperature of 200 °C. As-prepared CDs show a red emission around 615 nm with FWHM of 29 nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 12.8%.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), are new types of luminescent materials widely used for biological imaging [1–3]. As the most common method, solvothermal method usually produces CDs with broad fluorescent emission and poor color purity. Such CDs may have overlapped fluorescence with that of biological tissues, resulting in a low resolution of imaging. An effective way to improve the resolution of imaging is to use CDs with smaller FWHM [4–7]. FWHM is a tool for quantitative analysis of color purity by using the width at half of the fluorescence peak. Recently, several groups have reported the synthesis of narrow FWHM emissive CDs by using some special molecular precursors with a large π conjugated domain. For example, Xi et al. reported narrow FWHM emissive CDs synthesized from highly conjugated precursors of Tris(4-aminophenyl)amine [8]. Xie's group used 1,4-diaminonaphthalene as the precursor to synthesize CDs with narrow FWHM emission [9]. It is believable that the large π conjugated domains is beneficial for obtaining CDs with a single type of luminescence center, and thus realizing narrow FWHM emission [6]. A recent research hotspot is to find more new reactants capable of obtaining CDs with narrow FWHM emission. However, a question worth considering is whether there is some other ways to achieve narrow FWHM emission of CDs beside the use of a large π conjugated domain. As a typical prototype is p-phenylenediamine, which is a common molecular precursor for synthesis of CDs but never been reported for CDs with small FWHM emission below 40 nm [10,11]. It is curious that such ordinary and inexpensive reactant without an extremely large π conjugated domains is capable of synthesizing CDs with narrow FWHM emission.

Different from the liquid phase reaction, the solvent-free reaction does not need any solvent, which is more in line with the requirements of green synthesis. More importantly, because the reactants are not easy to diffuse in the solid phase, some new products that cannot be synthesized in the liquid phase can be generated by solvent-free reaction. Recently, solvent-free reaction has been used for preparation of CDs. For example, Xiong et al. reported the solvent-free synthesis of CDs with emission around 620 nm and FWHM over than 100 nm by mixing phloroglucinol molecular precursor with boric acid dispersant [12]. The same group also reported solvent-free synthesis of wide FWHM emissive CDs by the o-phenylenediamine precursor and KCl dispersant [13]. It is believed that the dispersants of either boric acid or KCl dispersant also plays a role of catalyzer, promoting the formation of CDs.

We herein reported the synthesis of CDs using p-phenylenediamine as sole precursor by a solvent-free and dispersant-free method. As-prepared CDs show a red emission around 615 nm and extremely narrow FWHM emission of 29 nm. More importantly, it is the first report about narrow FWHM emissive CDs with p-phenylenediamine which is a common raw material. Compared to previously reported solvent-free method using boric acid as dispersant [12], the emission of CDs prepared with solvent-free and dispersant-free method shows the narrower FWHM emission. In contrast solvothermal method, the current solvent-free method can prepare CDs with low content of amino groups on the surface of CDs, decreasing types of fluorescence centers and hence narrowing FWHM emission. Furthermore, in consideration of the extremely high cost for synthesis of CDs with narrow FWHM emission in previous work, the current solvent-free method is dozens of times lower cost due to the use inexpensive p-phenylenediamine and no solvent [8,14,15].

P-phenylenediamine (PPD, AR, 97%) and DMF (AR, 99.5%) were purchased from Macklin. Cyclohexane (AR) was purchased from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory, and ethanol (GR) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Boric acid (H3BO3,GR, ≥99.8%) was purchased from Aladdin. The 1000Da dialysis bag was purchased from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. MCF-7 cells were purchased from China Type Culture Collection. Cells were kept under standard cell culture condition (5% CO2, 37°C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs with a solvent-free and dispersant-free method (CDs-SF)
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 0.5g of p-phenylenediamine was ground for 10 min and then placed in a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE)-lined autoclave (25 mL). The autoclave was then heated for 6h in an incubator at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, crude solid CDs were obtained.

To remove the unreacted precursor and byproducts, the crude CDs were purified using dialysis. The typical method was to dissolve the crude CDs in DMF to obtain a dark brown solution, and then the solutions were further dialyzed against Deionized water through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 for 12 h. The suspensions in dialysis bag were further centrifuged at 10000 r·min−1 to obtain the precipitate. After removing the solvent and freeze-drying further, pure solid CDs-SF was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs at present of H3BO3 dispersant
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CDs at present of H3BO3 dispersant (symbolized by CDs- H3BO3) were synthesized by solvent-free method. Typically, 0.28 g boric acid and 0.5 g PPD were ground for 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 6 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature automatically, solid CDs-H3BO3 were obtained. Then CDs-H3PO3 was purified in a similar way to CDs-SF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs with solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: To make a comparison with the solvent-free method, CDs were also synthesized in a ethanol solvent (symbolized by CDs-EA) or a DMF solvent (symbolized by CDs-DMF) by using previously reported solvothermal method [10]. Typically, 0.9 g PPD was added to 8 mL ethanol (or DMF for CDs-DMF sample) under stirring about 10 min. Then the solution was transferred to a 25 mL poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE)-lined autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated in an incubator at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, CDs-EA and CDs-DMF were purified with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000. Then, CDs was dried and collected in a vacuum.

The cell imaging ability of CDs-SF was tested by MCF-7 cells. First, MCF-7 cells were placed in a culture dish and cultured in an incubator (37°C and 5% CO2) for 24 h to adhere. Then, cells were treated with CDs-SF aqueous solution (30 μg·mL−1) for 1-2 min. Finally, cells were imaged by the Zeiss Elyra P.1 super-resolution microscope with a 100 × Oil-immersion objective (λex = 561 nm, λem = 570-650 nm).

UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded with the L9 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer produced. PL and PLE (photoluminescence excitation) spectroscopy were measured using Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute PLQY of CDs was recorded using an Edinburgh instrument FS5 spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere at an excitation wavelength of 600 nm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) were measured using FEI Tecnai F20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using Thermos Scientific ESCAlab 250xi spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer. The FTIR samples were prepared by a tablet containing CDs and KBr. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using a Thermos scientific ESCAlab 250xi spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα as the X-ray source.

A solvent-free method was developed to prepare CDs with small emission FWHM. Briefly, the ground powder of p-phenylenediamine was heated for 6h at 200°C. After cooling and purification, CDs with emission peak at 615 nm, emission FWHM of 29 nm and PLQY of 12.8% were obtained (Scheme 1 ).

To obtain higher PLQY, the effects of reaction temperature, reaction time, and the amount of precursor on the fluorescence of CDs were studied. To make sure the same CDs concentration at different experimental condition, the absorbance values at 600nm of as-prepared CDs powders dissolved in DMF solvent was adjusted to 0.2. During PL measurement, the excitation wavelength was also set as 600 nm. In this way, the fluorescence intensity can be approximated as measured in the same concentration of carbon dot solution. It can be seen from Fig. 1 a-c that the optimal reaction temperature, the optimal mass of precursor, and the optimal reaction time are 200 °C, 0.5 g, and 6 h respectively. If no specific claiming, the above conditions are set as the default reaction conditions as following experiment.

Purification of CDs by dialysis is necessary to remove unreacted p-phenylenediamine. From Fig. S1, pure p-phenylenediamine has an absorption peak around 320 nm and an excitation wavelength-independent emission peak around 400 nm. When as-prepared CDs were not purified, both the absorption peak and emission peak from unreacted p-phenylenediamine can be observed as shown in Fig. S2. In comparison, after dialysis for 12 h, unreacted p-phenylenediamine had been completely removed. As can be seen from Fig. 1d, the purified CDs show a wide absorption band in the range of 400-600 nm and an excitation wavelength-independent emission peak around 615 nm with narrow FWHM of 29nm.

The morphology and structure of the CDs were characterized. From the TEM image (Fig. 1e), the prepared CDs are quasi-spherical and well dispersed, with an average particle size of 3.6 nm. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1f shows uniform lattice fringes with good resolution across the whole CDs with a lattice distance of 0.287 nm, corresponding to the graphene (020) plane [16]. The XRD result in Fig. 1g shows a broad diffraction peak near 21°, which is consistent with the typical graphene (002) plane [17,18], further confirming the formation of crystalline carbon [19,20].

The fluorescence spectra of CDs synthesized with different methods was compared. Fig. 2 showed spectra of CDs prepared with p-phenylenediamine by the current reported solvent-free and dispersant-free method, the literature-reported solvent-free method with boric acid as a dispersant, and solvothermal method in ethanol or DMF. The current solvent-free method without dispersant can synthesize CDs with FWHM emission of 29 nm (Fig. 2a). The CDs synthesized by both solvent-free method with dispersant and solvothermal method have a wide FWHM over than 40 nm, which is excited by the wavelength corresponding to the PLE peak (Fig. 2b-d). It confirms that the synthesis manner, which always be ignored in the past, is also an important factor determining the data of FWHM emission.

To study the origin of the narrow FWHM emission of CDs, the following control experiments or characterizations were proceeded. Firstly, in a control experiment, p-phenylenediamine were transferred into autoclave in a N2-filled glove box before heating and purification. In this case, the oxygen in the air can be eliminated. As shown in Fig. S4a, the prepared CDs are almost the same to that prepared at the atmospheric environment. It implies the oxidation of p-phenylenediamine is not the key factor affecting the FWHM of CDs in this work.

Thirdly, FTIR results in Fig. 4 also used to evaluate the contents of amino N in CDs prepared by different methods. The aryl ring stretching vibration at 1630-1450 cm−1 is used as a referent peak, the peak ratios of 3185 cm−1 (overtone of NH2 bending) with the peak of aryl ring stretching vibration at 1630-1450cm−1 were calculated [22]. The results show a lowest ratio for CDs prepared by the current solvent-free method (0.37), which is much smaller than that prepared by the literature-reported solvent-free method with boric acid as a dispersant (0.44), the solvothermal method in ethanol solution (0.60) and the solvothermal method at DMF solution (0.57). Obviously, FTIR results also suggest the lowest contents of amino N for CDs prepared by the current solvent-free method.
Finally, on the basis of the aforementioned results, it is confirmed that the less content of amino N in the surface of CDs is the key for the current solvent-free method to achieve CDs with narrow FWHM emission. Besides, theoretical calculation was used to investigate the mechanism of narrow FWHM emission of CDs. Fig. 5 shows the structure and spectra characters of CDs with different content of amino N by using a small π conjugated domain with eighteen carbon atoms under DFT (density functional theory) and TDDFT (time-dependent DFT) [23,24]. As can be seen, if no amino N connected on the π conjugated domain, all carbon atoms show a perfect plane structure with a narrow emission FWHM (39nm). When the number of connected amino N is different, data of emission FWHM change as NH2-1(46nm), NH2-2(50nm), NH2-3(54nm), NH2-4(54nm). Moreover, all carbon atoms are no longer in the same plane as that of molecule without NH2. Seen from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in Fig. S5, orbital distributions are different for with and without the amino N. The whole π conjugated domain are influenced by the amino N associating with broaden emission FWHM. Moreover, theoretical structures showed more twisted when more amino N groups connected to the same benzene ring. It is well known that the structure distortion promotes a stronger electron-phonon coupling strength, which determine the emission FWHM.

The formation mechanism of CDs by the current solvent-free and dispersant-free methods has been discussed. Compared with the solvothermal method, a key problem for the current solvent-free and dispersant-free method is how the precursor molecules diffuse and react together without the help of solvent. A fact is that the reactant (p-phenylenediamine) in this work has a low melting point of 139°C and hence presents with a molten state under the current reaction conditions of 200°C as confirmed by Fig. S6 in the supporting information. Therefore, the diffusion of p-phenylenediamine molecules becomes extremely easy, making the synthesis process of CDs quite similar to the traditional solvothermal method. Briefly, two steps are involved: melt of p-phenylenediamine from solid to liquid state and following by the formation of CDs by p-phenylenediamine through the well-known polymerization and carbonization processes (Fig. 6 ). Comparing FTIR and XPS results for the reactant (p-phenylenediamine) and the final production (CDs-SF), it is easy to deduce the formation of CDs by polymerization and carbonization of p-phenylenediamine. As shown by FTIR in Fig. S7, the -NH2 stretching vibrations (3346.8-3412.4 cm−1) and -NH2 wiggling (571.8-753.1 cm−1) in CDs-SF disappeared obviously, suggesting the reduction of amino nitrogen in the reactant of p-phenylenediamine and the formation of nitrogen-containing heterocycles in CDs-SF eventually. Simultaneously, in the high-resolution XPS spectra, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows the generation of pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, which indicates the formation of nitrogen-containing heterocycles in CD-SF. It is consistent with the conclusion of FTIR. Since there are no nitrogen-containing heterocycles in the sole reactant (p-phenylenediamine), the formation of nitrogen-containing heterocycles in CDs-SF can be used as a sign for the polymerization and carbonization of p-phenylenediamine.

As far as we know, this work is the first report about the synthesis of narrow FWHM emissive CDs by using p-phenylenediamine as a raw material. As shown in Table S2, the narrowest FWHM reported in the previous literature is about 61 nm by using p-phenylenediamine, far larger than the current results of 29 nm. As the most common used molecular precursor for CDs, p-phenylenediamine has a low cost. Table S3 lists the typical cost for narrow FWHM emissive CDs by using different molecular precursor. As can be seen, p-phenylenediamine is usually dozens of times lower than the currently used precursor with a large π conjugated domain.
Furthermore, in comparison to the solvothermal method, the free of solvent in the current work is not only has the merit of low cost, but also avoid the possible solvent-induced environment pollution. Moreover, the current solvent-free method can also be explored to other molecular precursors. As shown by Fig. S8, when o-phenylenediamine or m-phenylenediamine were used as the sole precursor, as-prepared CDs prepared by solvent-free method also show narrow FWHM of 34 nm or 32 nm respectively.

Since the prepared CDs-SF has red emission, it can better penetrate biological tissues for imaging [25,26,27]. The narrow FWHM emission of CDs also helps to distinguish them from the background fluorescence of biological tissues, making an improved imaging resolution. Fig. 7 shows the imaging of MCF-7 cells stained by CDs-SF. During cell imaging, the staining time is very short with only 1-2 min, which is shorter than that of most dyes. Moreover, the measured image does not use the water washing for the extracellular fluorescence is very weak. The possible reason is the weak fluorescence of the current CDs in water solvent but strong fluorescence in the interior of cells.

In conclusion, this work reported a solvent-free and dispersant-free method to synthesize narrow FWHM emissive CDs with p-phenylenediamine. As-prepared CDs show a red emission around 615 nm with FWHM only 29 nm. By comparing CDs prepared by either traditional solvothermal method or the solvent-free method, it is found that the solvent-free and dispersant-free method promotes a low content of amino groups on the surface of CDs, which benefits for reducing the types of fluorescence centers, and hence making CDs with narrow FWHM emission. The CDs-SF exhibit excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, making them as a biological dye for no-wash and rapid cell imaging. This work provides a new red narrow FWHM emissive nanomaterial for biomedical applications and optoelectronic devices. Moreover, it is confirmed that the narrow FWHM emission of CDs is correlated with the content of amino groups.

Hongcan Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuhong Xu: Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software. Zengwei Chen: Formal analysis. Fan Liu: Investigation. Shenfei Zong: Methodology. Zhuyuan Wang: Supervision. Chunlei Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 22075043, 21875034, 61704093).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2022.112092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbonized polymer dots: a brand new perspective to recognize luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep red emissive carbonized polymer dots with unprecedented narrow full width at half maximum

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li, B. Yang, Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications, ACS Central Sci.. 6 (2020) 2179-2195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, S. Lu, The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications, Matter 5 (2022) 110-149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational synthesis of highly efficient ultra-narrow red-emitting carbon quantum dots for NIR-II two-photon bioimaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel mechanism for red emission carbon dots: hydrogen bond dominated molecular states emission

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted facile synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon dots from o-phenylenediamine for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and H2O2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Song, Y. Cui, C. Zhang, Z. Hu, X. Liu, Microwave-assisted facile synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon dots from o-phenylenediamine for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and H2O2, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 17704-17712.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: S,N-Codoped oil-soluble fluorescent carbon dots for a high color-rendering WLED
